retrieved_docs.title: Delivery, Face and Brow Presentation -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: As cesarean delivery is becoming a more accessible mode of delivery in malpresentation, the incidence of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality during face presentation has dropped significantly. [14] However, some complications are still associated with the nature of labor in face presentation. Due to the fetal head position, engagement and descent of the head in the birth canal is more difficult, resulting in prolonged labor.

retrieved_docs.title: Delivery, Face and Brow Presentation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The vertex presentation, where the fetal neck is flexed to the chin, minimizing the head's circumference, is the most common presentation in term labor. Face presentation is an abnormal cephalic presentation where the mentum (chin) is the presenting part. This presentation typically occurs due to hyperextension of the fetal neck, with the occiput (back of the head) touching the fetal back. The incidence of a face malpresentation is rare, occurring in approximately 1 in 600 of all presentations. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Small for Gestational Age (Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Infants born with FGR SGA are at increased risk of short- and long-term complications and death. Conditions such as maternal hypertension, kidney disease, infections, malnutrition, smoking, and substance abuse increase the risk of having an FGR SGA infant. The evaluation of FGR SGA begins during the antenatal period. Pregnancies with FGR SGA fetuses can be detected with fundal height measurement and routine ultrasound exams during antenatal clinic visits. The early detection of fetuses at risk of FGR SGA helps in planning delivery and initial treatment. At birth, infants born with FGR SGA are thin, have loose peeling skin and decreased muscle mass, and may appear shrunken with wrinkled faces. In some, the head is larger than the rest of the body. Treatment of FGR SGA newborns is primarily focused on preventing and managing complications. Special attention is paid to the newborn’s temperature, and blood glucose is monitored and managed appropriately. Newborns needing more extensive care are admitted into the neonatal intensive care unit. In contrast, those with less need for monitoring can be kept in a mother’s room or a normal newborn nursery. The rate of death and disability is higher in infants born with lower gestational age and birth weight percentile.

retrieved_docs.title: Delivery, Face and Brow Presentation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Face presentation is an abnormal form of cephalic presentation caused by hyperextension of the fetal neck. Therefore, the mentum (chin) leads. This rare presentation occurs in about 1 in 600 deliveries. Face presentation requires careful assessment of the chin position when diagnosed through digital examination. Mentum-anterior orientation may allow for vaginal delivery, but mentum-posterior often necessitates a cesarean section due to obstructed descent through the birth canal.

retrieved_docs.title: Delivery, Face and Brow Presentation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Brow presentation occurs when the neck is less extended than in face presentation, with the presenting fetal part being the area between the anterior fontanelle and the orbital ridges. Brow presentation is considered the rarest form of malpresentation, with a prevalence of 1 in 500 to 1 in 4000 deliveries. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Delivery, Face and Brow Presentation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: These malpresentations are typically diagnosed during the second stage of labor via a digital examination. During the examination, it is possible to palpate the orbital ridges, nose, malar eminences, mentum, mouth, gums, and chin in cases of face presentation. Based on the chin's position, face presentation can be categorized as mentum anterior, posterior, or transverse. In brow presentation, the anterior fontanelle and face can be felt, but not the mouth and chin. Brow presentation can also be described based on the anterior fontanelle's position as frontal anterior, posterior, or transverse.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Many congenital facial anomalies have genetic causes, though some arise from preventable teratogen exposures. Developmental facial abnormalities can be disfiguring and devastating. The treatment of congenital facial disorders is often delayed until after birth because they are well-tolerated in utero . [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Small for Gestational Age (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: History starts from pre-conception through the antenatal and neonatal periods. Important points in the medical history include reviewing any chronic maternal conditions such as hypertension, renal disease, or diabetes mellitus; if the mother has an acute or chronic infection such as HIV, toxoplasmosis, rubella, or malaria; the mother’s nutritional status; and if substance use/abuse was present. Vaccination history and screening for antibody titers to ensure immunity to infections such as rubella and Cytomegalovirus should be determined. It is important to ask for a history of FGR SGA in prior pregnancies or the family history. Discussing the antenatal care and social stressors are important as well. Neonates with FGR SGA are usually thin, with loose peeling skin, decreased muscle mass, and loss of subcutaneous fat tissues. The face in these newborns is usually shrunken and wrinkled, and the umbilical cord is thin. The membranous bone formation in these infants is decreased, resulting in widened cranial sutures and larger than normal anterior fontanelle. The head appears relatively larger than the rest of the body in neonates with asymmetric FGR SGA.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Choanal atresia is a less common but potentially fatal congenital anomaly. The oronasal membrane (choana) normally recanalizes during fetal development. However, failure of this process can result in nasal obstruction by abnormal tissue. Choanal atresia may occur as part of CHARGE syndrome (see Image . CHARGE Syndrome). "CHARGE" stands for C oloboma, H eart disease, A tresia choanae, R etarded growth and development, G enital underdevelopment, and E ar anomalies. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Delivery, Face and Brow Presentation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: During vertex presentation, the fetal head flexes, bringing the chin to the chest, forming the smallest possible fetal head diameter, approximately 9.5 cm. With face and brow presentation, the neck hyperextends, resulting in greater cephalic diameters. As a result, the fetal head engages later, and labor progresses more slowly. Failure to progress in labor is also more common in both presentations compared to the vertex presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Facial developmental anomalies may be due to genetic or environmental causes. Pregnancy-related etiologies include fetal alcohol syndrome, uterine growth restriction, oligohydramnios, and maternal infections. [5] Genetic conditions that can cause abnormal facial development include Pierre-Robin, Treacher-Collins, Fragile X, Down, and DiGeorge syndromes. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Small for Gestational Age (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The routine ultrasound examination is another screening method for FGR SGA, but there is no agreement on the timing and number of screenings required. When 2 screenings are obtained, the exams are usually performed at 32 and 36 weeks of gestation, while for a single screening, the exam is performed between 32 and 36 weeks of gestation. [31] [32] In many countries, including the United States, serial fundal height measurement and risk assessment for impaired fetal growth, such as the history of maternal chronic disease, substance use disorder, smoking, and placental insufficiency, are recommended for each antenatal visit. [4] In pregnancy with a high risk for FGR SGA, an ultrasound is performed once or twice in the 3rd trimester or when a lag in fundal height is detected. However, in FGR SGA, low-risk pregnancies, ultrasound is only performed in any of the following: a lag in fundal height, inability to palpate fundal height, or fundal height not reliable as in the case of a uterine mass. [33] [34] Postnatal Diagnosis: FGR SGA is diagnosed in postnatal life when the birth weight is less than the 10th percentile for gestational age. Other features in support of FGR SGA include physical evidence of malnutrition, such as decreased skeletal muscles, subcutaneous fat tissues, and loose peeling skin. Accurate determination of gestational age is important in making a reliable diagnosis of FGR SGA. The 2 reliable methods used in assessing the fetus's gestational age are calculated using the date of the last menstrual period or the measurement from a prenatal ultrasound performed before a gestational age of 20 weeks. [35] In situations where either the correct menstrual date or prenatal ultrasound is not obtainable, gestational age can be determined after birth using the physical and neuromuscular assessments of the neonate using the Ballard exam. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft lip and palate, one of the most common congenital craniofacial abnormalities, is characterized by failure of normal fusion of the palate and lip during development, resulting in a clinically evident discontinuity of the lip or palate at birth. Cleft lip and palate (CL/P) and cleft palate alone (CPO) are not only cosmetic deformities but have significant functional morbidity for the newborn without adequate management. CL/P impacts the newborn's ability to feed in multiple ways, including increased nasal reflux, inability to form an adequate latch, and increased work of feeding, leading to fatigue.

retrieved_docs.title: Birth Trauma -- Issues of Concern -- Physical Birth Trauma
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for physical birth trauma include the following: Fetal and pregnancy-related factors Macrosomia Macrocephaly Very low birth weight Extreme prematurity Congenital anomalies Oligohydramnios and malpresentation, including breech presentation as well as other abnormal presentations (eg, the face, brow, or transverse) Maternal factors Obesity Preexisting medical conditions, including diabetes or hypertension Medical conditions diagnosed during pregnancy, including diabetes or hypertension Cephalopelvic disproportion Small maternal stature Nulliparity Labor dystocia and difficult fetal extraction Use of vacuum or forceps Prolonged or rapid labor [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Craniofacial Growth, And Development -- Development -- Face
retrieved_docs.content: Facial formation in the fetus occurs between the 5th and 10th week and relies on the fusion of five mesenchymal swellings: the frontonasal prominence, two mandibular processes, and two maxillary processes. Development of the face relies on the growth and fusion of these prominences, and failure can result in various facial abnormalities, some of which I will discuss in the clinical significance section. The maxillary and mandibular processes arise from the first pharyngeal arch (which explains why the sensation of the face is supplied by the trigeminal nerve, which is the first arch’s associated nerve). Neural crest mesenchyme gives rise to the frontonasal prominence.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Measurement of Growth
retrieved_docs.content: Head circumference or occipitofrontal circumference: Head circumference is assessed by measuring the largest area from the prominent site at the back (occiput) to the frontal prominence above the supraorbital ridge. Brain growth is maximum in the first 3 years of life, so head circumference is used in children under 3 years.  It is measured as the maximum diameter through the supraorbital ridge to the occiput and documented to the closest 0.01 cm. Microcephaly is more than 2 standard deviations below the mean. Macrocephaly is more than 2 standard deviations above the mean.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the model demonstrates that gestational age, birth weight, and fetal growth metrics intersect with diagnostic classifications, perinatal disease categories, and health‑service utilization, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, classifying, and managing conditions that arise around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: also underlies dyspareunia (painful sexual intercourse), which is classified under the same “Other specified female genital disorders” concept. In the pediatric context, infants born preterm with low birth weight have an increased risk of short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature itself is associated with higher rates of neonatal death. Unspecified obstetric codes for care or intervention encompass unspecified malposition or malpresentation, reflecting the hierarchical relationship between broad labor‑delivery indications and specific obstetric complications. Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how structural cervical weakness, congenital pelvic anomalies, prior surgical scars, and associated fetal‑maternal outcomes converge to inform risk assessment, diagnosis, and targeted management in maternal‑fetal medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 547
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION” is defined as a parent category that includes the combined condition “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation.” This combined condition is used as both a descriptor and a classification label for itself, emphasizing its role as a distinct clinical grouping. The specific diagnosis “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal undernutrition without reference to being light or small for gestational age) is classified under the “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” grouping, indicating that it shares the same underlying features of premature delivery, reduced birth weight, and impaired fetal growth. In addition, “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” has a broader relationship to the Arabic‑language concept “اضطراب لدى الأطفال وحديثي الولادة,” which denotes a disorder affecting infants and newborns. Together, these relationships illustrate how fetal growth restriction, prematurity, and low birth weight are interconnected components of fetal malnutrition and form part of a larger spectrum of neonatal disorders, underscoring the importance of recognizing these linked conditions for comprehensive perinatal assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 260
retrieved_docs.content: The knowledge network describes a hierarchical perinatal classification in which a gestational period of 29–30 weeks defines a live‑born infant (خديج) and places the infant within the broader “liveborn” category; gestational length is an inverse‑isa attribute that determines live‑born status. Disorders associated with short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation (short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation) are children of “CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION” and are linked to “تقييد النمو الجنيني” (fetal growth restriction), “ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}” (birth at extreme prematurity, weight unspecified), and “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal undernutrition without mention of light or small for gestational age). These conditions are also associated with perinatal infections (“عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك”) and with the generic “حالة غير محددة

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.
